Executive Summary

Executive Summary

This is a VMC wind day for the western US — the active weather is entirely confined to the Northeast and Mid-Atlantic, well clear of your route. Primary Risk: wind over the Cascades and high terrain near Bend, with possible light mountain wave turbulence at 12,000 ft on the Oregon approach. Both departure and arrival AFDs confirm VFR with light winds, and the surface prog shows a strong Pacific High dominating California and the Pacific Northwest through your flight window. This is a GO with high confidence — the main thing to watch is ride quality over the terrain transition from the Cascades into Bend, not weather avoidance.

What Forecasters Are Saying

  • MTR (San Carlos departure): Confident VFR throughout. West winds building to breezy in the afternoon, likely staying below 20 kts at SFO-area. No ceiling concerns en route departure.
  • PDT (Bend arrival): High confidence VFR through the entire TAF period. Winds light and variable, under 10 kts. No forecast concerns whatsoever.
  • Terrain trigger: Neither AFD flags mountain wave or significant terrain-driven turbulence — but neither AFD covers the Cascade crest directly, so that gap in coverage is worth noting.
  • System weather: Forecasters note all precipitation and clouds are locked to the Northeast. Zero mention of Pacific moisture affecting California or Oregon during your window.

Operational Briefing

The Day Before — What to Watch

Sunday evening is a non-event for your route. The surface progs show the Pacific High firmly in place over the West, with no approaching fronts or troughs anywhere near California or Oregon through at least Tuesday. The active low and precipitation field are plastered against the East Coast and stay there.

Your Sunday night check should focus on:

  • Updated winds aloft for 9,000–12,000 ft at SFO, SAC, and RDM — looking for any significant increase in the westerly flow that could signal stronger mountain wave over the Cascades
  • Check for any SIGMET or G-AIRMET for mountain obscuration or moderate turbulence over the Oregon Cascades — these sometimes pop up with overnight model runs even on otherwise quiet days
  • A KBDN METAR in the morning — not because the TAF concerns you, but to confirm the desert air is dry and calm as advertised

Nothing on these charts would change the go/no-go tonight. The only thing that could push this to marginal is a significantly stronger-than-forecast wind aloft over the Cascades. That's a low-probability watch item, not an action item.

Leaving KSQL — Departure at 2026-03-23 14:00Z

Departure at 14Z (6am local Pacific) is clean. The SFO TAF valid through your departure shows SKC, P6SM, with west winds around 7–9 kts. The MTR AFD confirms VFR across the Bay Area at that hour, with coastal stratus already eroding by mid-morning. At 14Z you're right in that transition window — you may see patchy low marine layer along the immediate coast and Monterey Bay, but the TAF for SFO itself shows clear skies.

San Carlos is 9 nm from SFO — expect similar conditions: SKC, unrestricted visibility, light westerly surface wind. Climb-out will be in VMC. No layers to punch through, no IMC concern on departure.

The SAC winds aloft at 3,000 ft show essentially calm/light (9900 = calm/light variable), turning to 270°/13 kts at 6,000 ft. You'll feel a light headwind component building as you climb northbound and then northeast toward the Cascades, but nothing dramatic out of the Bay Area.

The Ride at 12,000 ft

Icing

No significant icing signal present along this route. The QPF charts show zero precipitation over California and Oregon during your flight window. The surface progs confirm no moisture-bearing systems anywhere near the West Coast. Freezing level at 12,000 ft over the Central Valley and Cascades foothills is right around your cruise altitude based on the SFO temps aloft (+4°C at 12,000 ft), meaning you're near the 0°C isotherm — but with no cloud field or moisture indicated, there is nothing to freeze on.

Over the Cascades and into Bend, the RDM data shows -3°C at 12,000 ft — you'll be flying in sub-freezing air. But again, no moisture = no icing. The sky is forecast clear by both AFDs. This is dry high-desert winter air.

AltitudeIcing RiskNotes
Below 8,000 ftNoneAbove freezing, no moisture
9,000–12,000 ftMinimal / LOW CONFIDENCENear 0°C over terrain, but no cloud field forecast
Above 12,000 ftNoneClear, dry air mass

No SLD signal present. Not a factor for this flight.

Winds Aloft & Ride Quality

Here's the winds aloft picture decoded for your route at 12,000 ft (FL120), valid 23/06Z — closest useful data for your 14Z–16:30Z window:

Station12,000 ft WindTempRoute Impact
SFO290°/27 kts+4°CDeparture — light crosswind/tailwind climbing NE
SAC280°/27 kts+3°CCentral Valley — crosswind, moderate westerly
RDM (Redmond)240°/19 kts-3°CNear Bend — light headwind on final approach

On the SFO/SAC side, the 280–290° flow at 27 kts at 12,000 ft is essentially a direct crosswind to slight tailwind as you track northeast. That's not bad — you'll get a slight push early in the flight. As you transition over the Cascades and approach Redmond/Bend, the flow backs to 240° and lightens to ~19 kts, giving you a modest headwind on the descent and approach into KBDN.

Ride quality: With 25–27 kt westerlies at 12,000 ft crossing the Sierra Nevada and then the Cascades, expect LOCALIZED, CONDITIONAL moderate turbulence over the ridgelines — particularly as you cross the Cascades on the approach to Bend. This is a terrain day, not a system day. Mountain wave is possible but not confirmed by any turbulence guidance in the provided charts. The eTCF charts show convectively-driven turbulence only over the Northeast — zero signal over the West. The Cascades turbulence is a mechanical/terrain consideration.

Below the ridgelines (~8,000 ft) the flow lightens and the ride should smooth out. If you get a rough patch over the Cascades crest, descending to 10,000 ft may help, though terrain clearance must be respected — the Cascades top out near 9–10,000 ft in the Bend area.

Other Hazards

HazardRiskLegWhat to Expect

Getting Into KBDN

ETA Bend is approximately 16:29Z (8:29am local). The PDT AFD is as clean as it gets: "VFR conditions persist through the forecast period. Winds will remain light and variable under 10 kts." The surface prog for Mon 12Z shows the Pacific High building over the Great Basin with Bend sitting comfortably under high pressure.

This is a non-event VMC arrival. No fronts, no moisture, no precipitation anywhere near central Oregon during your window.

Arrival Wind Analysis — KBDN

Surface winds at KBDN are forecast light and variable, under 10 kts. The winds aloft at RDM show 240°/19 kts at 12,000 ft, lightening on descent. By the time you're at pattern altitude (~4,000 ft MSL, ~500 ft AGL), expect surface winds consistent with the TAF — likely southwest at 5–8 kts or less, possibly variable. KBDN's primary runway is 16/34; with a light southwesterly surface wind, Runway 34 gives you a slight left crosswind or near-direct headwind component. No crosswind concerns. Note: terrain can modify winds locally at Bend — treat surface wind as variable until you have a current METAR.

Alternates: KRDM (Redmond, 17 nm north) is the obvious alternate — towered, ILS, services. Non-event day so you won't need it, but file it.

Fly or No

GO.

Hard stops: None. There are no IMC concerns, no icing, no convection, no fronts anywhere near this route.

The only operational watch item is moderate turbulence over the Cascade crest — a realistic possibility with 25+ kt westerly flow at cruise altitude crossing high terrain. That's manageable. Tighten your belt, slow to maneuvering speed over the ridges if it gets rough, and know that it will smooth out once you're on the east side descending toward Bend.

This flight goes. Don't overthink it.

If You Go — Do This

  1. Check winds aloft one more time Sunday night — specifically the RDM 9,000 and 12,000 ft forecast. If winds over the Cascades are cranking above 35–40 kts, reconsider altitude or timing.
  2. File IFR for routing and terrain structure, not because you expect IMC. You're crossing 9–10,000 ft terrain in the dark — IFR keeps you separated and gives you options. VFR is legal but IFR is smarter here.
  3. Cruise 12,000 ft as planned. You're right at the freezing level with no moisture — not a concern. The altitude gives you good terrain clearance over the Cascades.
  4. Slow to Va over the Cascade ridgelines if you pick up moderate chop. Don't fight it.
  5. Get a current KBDN METAR before descent — confirm winds are as advertised before committing to a runway.
  6. File KRDM as alternate — formality, not necessity.
  7. Morning of: check for any G-AIRMETs for mountain obscuration or moderate turbulence over Oregon Cascades, issued around 06Z–12Z Monday. That's your last meaningful data check before wheels-up.

Confidence

Confidence: HIGH for the go/no-go call. Both AFDs agree, the charts are consistent, and the Pacific High is firmly in control of the western US through at least Tuesday. No conflicting model signals, no approaching systems.

Confidence: MEDIUM on the specific ride quality over the Cascades. The 25 kt westerly flow at 12,000 ft is real and the terrain is real — but whether that translates to light chop, moderate turbulence, or smooth air depends on wave mechanics that aren't fully resolved at this range. It's the one unknown. Plan for bumpy over the ridge; hope for smooth.

Nothing in the forecast picture changes the go/no-go for this flight. The weather is happening in another timezone.
